critic_raw:
This deep RL vs. ETS inventory management design has several critical flaws centered on the offline RL training procedure and the fairness of comparison.

The RL agent is described as using PPO — an on-policy algorithm — trained 'offline' on a 14-month historical dataset with 'experience replay.' PPO with experience replay is not a standard implementation. PPO is on-policy: its theoretical guarantees depend on collecting fresh rollouts from the current policy and updating within a clip ratio. Using experience replay from a fixed historical dataset violates the on-policy assumption and can produce biased policy gradient estimates. The actual algorithm being run is either offline RL (which should use different algorithms like CQL or IQL) or PPO run on stale trajectories (which is methodologically incorrect). This is not a minor implementation detail — the stability and convergence guarantees of PPO do not apply in this configuration.

All preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, including the test period (months 19-24). The design explicitly states this and justifies it by 'enhancing the model's robustness to shifts in data distribution.' This reasoning is backwards: fitting on the full dataset including the test period allows the STL seasonality decomposition, normalization parameters, and categorical encodings to be informed by future data. For a temporal split where months 19-24 are meant to simulate deployment conditions, this is leakage. The STL decomposition is particularly problematic: it identifies seasonal components over the full time series, so the seasonal adjustment applied to training data incorporates information about the seasonal pattern present in the test period.

The comparison between PPO (tuned with grid search over learning rate, entropy coefficient, and clip ratio) and ETS (tuned with grid search to minimize composite validation reward) is not apples-to-apples. ETS has at most 3 smoothing parameters; PPO has a neural network policy with ~50-dimensional state space and many more degrees of freedom. Even with matched grid search effort, the hypothesis cannot be attributed to RL policy learning vs. ETS structure — it is confounded by model capacity.

The composite reward weights (1.0 × stockout + 1.5 × spoilage + 0.2 × cost) are chosen before training, which is correctly noted. However, the evaluation metric is defined as the same weighted combination. Optimizing and evaluating on identical composites means the experiment measures consistency with the assumed trade-off, not performance on any single operational metric. If the true operator preference differs from the 1.5 weight on spoilage, the RL agent will appear optimal even when it produces outcomes worse on every individual metric than the ETS baseline.

The test period covers months 19-24, which is 6 months and likely spans the holiday season (if month 1 is January, months 19-24 are July-December including holiday peak). The training period (months 1-14) also contains some seasonal variation but may underrepresent peak holiday demand. The design notes this as a potential seasonal confound and offers a sensitivity analysis, but this is a post-hoc acknowledgment rather than a design control — the primary analysis may be confounded by a distributional shift the RL agent was never trained to handle.
all_issues_raised: PPO is an on-policy algorithm; training it 'offline with experience replay' on historical data violates PPO's theoretical assumptions and produces biased policy gradient estimates — this is not a valid offline RL procedure.

All preprocessing transformers including STL seasonality decomposition are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-period seasonal structure into the training features despite the temporal split design.

The comparison confounds RL policy learning with model capacity: PPO has a high-capacity neural policy while ETS has 3 smoothing parameters — differences in outcome cannot be attributed to the RL mechanism alone.

The reward function and primary evaluation metric use identical composite weights, so the experiment measures consistency with assumed trade-off preferences rather than performance on operational outcomes — a model optimizing the wrong trade-off would appear optimal.

The test period likely corresponds to peak seasonal demand (months 19-24) that is underrepresented in training data; the RL agent is evaluated on distribution shift conditions it was not trained to handle, introducing a confound favoring or penalizing RL in ways unrelated to the hypothesis.
